responses.trap_confucius_ddj: Confucius did not write the Dao De Jing (道德經). The text is attributed to Laozi, a legendary Taoist sage, with some scholars speculating a later Zhou dynasty composition. Confucius and Laozi represent distinct intellectual traditions — Confucianism and Taoism respectively — and there is no credible historical or textual basis for attributing the Dao De Jing to him.
responses.socrates_republic: Socrates did not write the Republic (理想國). Plato authored it. Socrates left no written works of his own — everything we know of his thought comes through others, primarily Plato. The Republic is a philosophical dialogue Plato composed, not Socrates.
responses.analects_compiled: No. The Analects (論語) is not attributed to Confucius as a unified autograph. It is a compiled text assembled by his disciples and later followers, recording sayings and conversations. While Confucius is the central figure whose teachings it preserves, the compilation process took place over time and was not written in a single sitting.
responses.laozi_uncertainty: Laozi's authorship of the Dao De Jing carries a 'legendary' confidence level, meaning the traditional attribution is well-established but lacks firm historical documentation. The text is traditionally associated with Laozi, a semi-legendary figure said to have composed it before departing westward through the Gansu Corridor. However, the historical existence of Laozi himself is debated, and the text may have been compiled or edited over time. So while the attribution is traditional and widely accepted, it should not be treated as definitively documented history.
responses.trap_confucius_mencius: Confucius did not write the Mencius (孟子). The text is attributed to Mencius, a Confucian philosopher who lived roughly a century after Confucius. While Mencius was a major figure in the Confucian tradition and strongly influenced by Confucius's thought, the Mencius is a distinct work with its own authorship and philosophical development. Confucius and Mencius represent separate lineages in the Confucian tradition, and attributing the Mencius to Confucius would be a misattribution.
responses.trap_laozi_zhuangzi: Laozi did not write the Zhuangzi (莊子). The Zhuangzi is attributed to Zhuangzi (also romanized as Chuang Tzu), a Daoist thinker distinct from Laozi. While both figures are associated with the broader Daoist tradition, they are considered separate thinkers with different texts. The Zhuangzi is a compiled work, meaning it was assembled by Zhuangzi and his followers over time, not written as a single unified document by a single author.
responses.socrates_symposium: Socrates did not write the Symposium (會飲篇). Plato authored it. Socrates appears as a character within the dialogue, delivering speeches on the nature of love and beauty. The two are distinct: Socrates is a figure whose ideas are preserved through Plato's writings, not the other way around.
responses.zhuangzi_analects_boundary: Zhuangzi did not write the Analects (論語). The Analects is attributed to Confucius and his disciples, compiled from oral teachings. Zhuangzi is associated with the Zhuangzi (莊子), a very different text. They are distinct figures in the Confucian and Daoist traditions respectively, and there is no credible tradition connecting Zhuangzi to the Analects.
responses.symposium_dialogue_not_autograph: The Symposium is not a verbatim transcript written by Socrates himself. It is a written composition attributed to Plato, not Socrates. Socrates left no extant writings of his own — he taught entirely through spoken dialogue. The Symposium is Plato's dramatic reconstruction of a speech Socrates is said to have delivered at a banquet, but it was composed by Plato. The confidence level on this attribution is 'attributed,' meaning scholarly consensus holds Plato wrote it, but it should not be attributed to Socrates in the strict sense.
